## Executive summary

In 2024/25, 19.0% of full-time academic staff at the University of Glasgow were employed on teaching-only contracts, compared with 13.5% for the Russell Group excluding Glasgow (23 institutions) and 20.7% for non-Russell Group institutions. Glasgow ranked 7th among the 24 Russell Group institutions on this measure in 2024/25. Over the period 2014/15 to 2024/25, the teaching-only share rose at all three comparators, with the Russell Group excluding Glasgow showing the largest proportional increase. This analysis uses HESA Table 17, which covers full-time staff only. Across all higher education providers in 2024/25, the Spearman correlation between total student enrolments and teaching-only share is rho = -0.32 (n = 219, p = < .001); the all-provider regression predicts a teaching-only share of 17.4% for Glasgow against an observed share of 19.0%.

## Background and question

This brief examines the proportion of full-time academic staff at the University of Glasgow employed on teaching-only contracts, and compares that proportion with the Russell Group and the wider higher education sector. The analysis addresses the question of where Glasgow sits relative to its Russell Group peers and to the wider sector, and how that position has changed over the decade from 2014/15 to 2024/25. HESA Table 17 is used because it is the only HESA open dataset that supports comparison at the individual institution level.

## Data and methods

The analysis draws on HESA Table 17, which covers full-time academic staff (excluding atypicals) across all terms of employment. Counts are Full-Person Equivalent (FPE), rounded to the nearest 5. The dataset spans academic years 2014/15 to 2024/25 (11 files).

Staff are included if they fall into one of three academic employment functions (teaching only; research only; both teaching and research) and one of three contract levels (professor; other senior academic; other contract level). Staff reported under "Neither teaching nor research" are excluded. Sex is restricted to Female and Male; staff reported as Other or Not known are excluded. From 2022/23, HESA changed the basis for recording sex from self-identification to legal documents, which may introduce a small discontinuity in the time series.

Three comparator groups are defined consistently across all analyses. "University of Glasgow" refers to rows where `he_provider` is "The University of Glasgow". "Russell Group (excluding Glasgow)" comprises the remaining 23 Russell Group institutions as recorded in HESA Table 17. "Non-Russell Group" comprises all other institutions. Because Table 17 contains separate rows for each contract salary band as well as a total, and separate rows for each country and region combination, all analyses filter to `contract_salary == "Total"`, `country_of_he_provider == "All"`, and `region_of_he_provider == "All"` to avoid double-counting. The primary metric throughout is the percentage of academic staff whose `academic_employment_function` is "teaching only", with the denominator being all staff in the three functions listed above.

**Table 17 covers full-time staff only.** This is the most important caveat for interpreting these figures. Part-time staff are excluded from all analyses in this brief.

Figure 1 shows the percentage of full-time academic staff on teaching-only contracts across all three comparator groups from 2014/15 to 2024/25. In 2024/25 the University of Glasgow stood at 19.0%, the Russell Group excluding Glasgow at 13.5%, and non-Russell Group institutions at 20.7%. All three groups show an upward trend across the period. In 2014/15 the University of Glasgow (9.7%) was positioned between the Russell Group excluding Glasgow (7.7%) and non-Russell Group institutions (10.3%); by 2024/25 this ordering was maintained, with Glasgow sitting approximately midway between the two comparators.

Figure 2 shows the percentage of full-time academic staff on teaching-only contracts for each of the 24 Russell Group institutions in 2024/25, sorted in descending order. The University of Glasgow ranked 7th out of 24, with 19.0% of staff on teaching-only contracts. The Russell Group mean excluding Glasgow was 15.2% and the median was 16.0%, placing Glasgow approximately 3.8 percentage points above the mean and 3.0 percentage points above the median. The lowest-ranked institutions (University of Oxford at 1.6% and University of Cambridge at 3.8%) are substantially below the group average.

The tables above show the teaching-only percentage and total staff count for each contract level and comparator group in 2024/25. At professor level, Glasgow's teaching-only share (7.2%) is approximately twice that of the Russell Group excluding Glasgow (3.3%) and non-Russell Group institutions (3.5%). At other contract level, the differences are smaller: Glasgow stands at 22.3%, compared with 15.7% for the Russell Group excluding Glasgow and 23.2% for non-Russell Group institutions. The other senior academic category is very small at Glasgow (15 FPE), which limits the reliability of the percentage for that level.

The table above shows teaching-only percentages for each group in 2014/15 and 2024/25 and the percentage-point change over the decade. The University of Glasgow increased by 9.3 percentage points (9.7% to 19.0%). The Russell Group excluding Glasgow increased by 5.9 percentage points (7.7% to 13.5%). Non-Russell Group institutions increased by 10.5 percentage points (10.3% to 20.7%).

The table above shows the full academic employment function mix for each comparator group in 2024/25. Glasgow's higher teaching-only share (19.0%) is accompanied by a higher research-only share (33.9%) than the non-Russell Group institutions (21.3%), though slightly lower than the Russell Group excluding Glasgow (42.2%). Glasgow's both-teaching-and-research share (47.1%) is similar to the Russell Group excluding Glasgow (44.3%) and substantially higher than the non-Russell Group (58.0%). The non-Russell Group has a markedly lower research-only share, which reflects the different institutional missions in that group.

## Institutional size and teaching-only share


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![Figure 3. Total student enrolments (log scale) and percentage of full-time academic staff on teaching-only contracts, all HE providers, 2024/25](glasgow-teaching-only-brief_files/figure-docx/fig-student-scatter-1.png){#fig-student-scatter}
:::
:::



::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![Figure 4. Student-to-staff ratio and percentage of full-time academic staff on teaching-only contracts, all HE providers, 2024/25](glasgow-teaching-only-brief_files/figure-docx/fig-ssr-scatter-1.png){#fig-ssr-scatter}
:::
:::


Figure 3 shows the relationship between total student enrolments and the percentage of full-time academic staff on teaching-only contracts, across all HE providers in 2024/25. The x-axis uses a log scale to accommodate the wide range of institutional sizes. Spearman's rank correlation is rho = -0.32 (n = 219, p = < .001). Within the Russell Group alone, the correlation is rho = 0.06 (n = 24, p = 0.784). Given Glasgow's student population, the all-provider regression predicts a teaching-only share of 17.4%; Glasgow's observed share is 19.0%, 1.6 percentage points above the predicted value. No statistical claim is made about whether this residual is significant; the comparison is descriptive only.

Figure 4 reports the same analysis using the student-to-staff ratio in place of raw student numbers, providing a more direct proxy for teaching workload than institutional size alone. Spearman's rank correlation between student-to-staff ratio and teaching-only share is rho = 0.04 (n = 219, p = 0.527) across all providers, and rho = 0.70 (n = 24, p = < .001) within the Russell Group. Both analyses are cross-sectional and descriptive; a correlation between student numbers and teaching-only share could reflect institutional size, teaching workload, strategic staffing choices, or confounding variation in institutional type, and the data cannot distinguish between these explanations.

## Limitations

- **Full-time staff only.** Table 17 covers full-time academic staff. Part-time staff are excluded from all figures in this brief. Because women are more likely than men to work part-time, the figures likely understate the teaching-only share among female staff and may not be directly comparable to analyses based on all-mode employment data.
- **FPE rounding.** HESA rounds counts to the nearest 5 FPE. This introduces noise, particularly for institutions with small numbers of staff in a given category. Percentage figures at the institutional level should be interpreted with appropriate caution.
- **Classification consistency.** Cross-institutional comparison assumes consistent classification of `academic_employment_function` across institutions. In practice, the boundary between "teaching only" and "both teaching and research" may be drawn differently at different universities.
- **Small cells.** Some cells, particularly at the "other senior academic" contract level for Glasgow (15 FPE total in 2024/25), are very small. Percentages for such cells are unreliable and should not be over-interpreted.
- **Descriptive analysis.** This analysis is descriptive. The figures show patterns in the data and do not establish causation or explain why institutions differ.
- **Cross-sectional correlation.** Cross-sectional descriptive analysis cannot distinguish between institutional size, teaching workload, and strategic choice as drivers of any observed relationship between student numbers and teaching-only share.
